name: Government Effectiveness
source: World Bank, The Worldwide Governance Indicators
comments: Government effectiveness score 80.06, rank 17/135.
insights: 🟡 Moderate gap vs Singapore (100.00): 19.9 points behind. | ℹ️ Brunei scores well on government effectiveness (80.06, rank 17), though Singapore posts a perfect 100.00.
analysis: Government Effectiveness: Brunei scores ~90th percentile (WGI absolute score 90.09 in 2023, per World Bank DataBank) in WGI 2025. Reflects effective public service delivery anchored by a professional civil service. Limitations include low private sector participation in governance and limited accountability mechanisms. Civil service reform under JPA (Public Service Department, jpa.gov.bn) and IPA (Institut Perkhidmatan Awam) training programmes, aligned with Wawasan 2035, contributing to stable scores.

name: Rule of Law
comments: Rule of law score 73.77, rank 29/135.
insights: 🟡 Moderate gap vs Singapore (94.81): 21.0 points behind. | ℹ️ Solid rule-of-law performance (73.77, rank 29); meaningful distance remains to Singapore's 94.81.
analysis: Rule of Law: Brunei scores ~73rd percentile. Strong legal framework rooted in English common law tradition and Syariah law for Muslims. Gaps in judicial independence perception (no jury trials) and enforcement consistency in commercial disputes reduce the score relative to regional peers like Singapore.

name: Political Stability
comments: Political stability score 98.60, rank 2/135 — one of Brunei's strongest indicators.
insights: 🟢 Small gap vs Singapore (100.00): 1.4 points behind. | ℹ️ Brunei's political stability score (98.60) is exceptionally strong — rank 2 globally, just ahead of Singapore.
analysis: Political Stability & Absence of Violence: Brunei's strongest WGI indicator — near-perfect score. Sultanate governance model, absence of political parties, and extremely low civil unrest or terrorism contribute. Consistent historically; one of the most stable countries globally. Key strength for talent retention.

name: Regulatory Quality
comments: Regulatory quality score 69.28, rank 31/135.
insights: 🔴 Large gap vs Singapore (100.00): 30.7 points behind. | ℹ️ Regulatory quality (69.28, rank 31) trails Singapore's maximum score; an area for targeted reform.
analysis: Regulatory Quality: ~69th percentile — moderate. Business licensing has improved (Darussalam Enterprise reforms), but regulatory burden in non-oil sectors, FDI entry barriers, and limited regulatory transparency reduce score. ASEAN peers Malaysia and Thailand score higher. Brunei's regulatory reform agenda under BDCB and Business Licensing Centre is ongoing.

name: Corruption Perceptions Index
comments: Not reported in 2025 GTCI for Brunei.
insights: ⚠️ Not reported for Brunei in GTCI 2025 (Corruption Perceptions Index 2024 not yet integrated for this economy).
analysis: Corruption Perceptions: CPI 2025 (published Feb 2026) — Brunei scored 63, ranked 31st globally. Up from 60 (35th) in CPI 2024. Ranks 2nd in ASEAN behind Singapore (84). Brunei's Anti-Corruption Bureau (ACB) active investigations and asset declaration requirements contribute positively. Areas for improvement: procurement transparency and whistleblower protection.

name: Extent of Market Dominance
comments: Extent of market dominance score 39.42, rank 99/135 — concentrated market structure.
insights: 🟡 Moderate gap vs Singapore (64.60): 25.2 points behind. | ℹ️ Market concentration remains high (39.42, rank 99); competition policy is a priority area.
analysis: Market Competition Intensity: WEF EOS 2025 score 39.42/100. Business leaders perceive moderate-low market competition. Oil & gas sector dominance, limited SME ecosystem, and few large domestic conglomerates create concentration. Wawasan 2035 economic diversification aims to create more competitive market conditions across manufacturing, halal, and digital sectors.

name: Domestic Credit to Private Sector
source: IMF International Financial Statistics, World Bank WDI
comments: Domestic credit to private sector score 15.42, rank 76/135 — constrained business financing access.
insights: 🔴 Large gap vs Singapore (63.79): 48.4 points behind. | ℹ️ Domestic credit to the private sector is constrained (15.42, rank 76), pointing to limited business financing access.
analysis: Domestic Credit to Private Sector: ~38% of GDP. IMF 2024 Article IV confirms bank lending to private sector rose 3.8% YoY in 2023 but remains structurally low. GTCI normalised score 15.42/100 reflects how far below global norms Brunei sits. SME financing remains constrained; BDCB credit guarantee schemes partially addressing gap. Islamic finance expansion could unlock additional credit channels.

name: Cluster Development
comments: Cluster development score 44.29, rank 82/135 — limited industrial clusters; low diversification.
insights: 🟡 Moderate gap vs Singapore (66.22): 21.9 points behind. | ℹ️ Cluster development is limited (44.29, rank 82), reflecting low industrial diversification.
analysis: Cluster Development: WEF EOS 2025 score 44.29/100. Oil & gas cluster (Seria/Kuala Belait corridor) is the most developed. Digital and tech clusters nascent — ITPSS (Anggerek Desa Technology Park) developing. Halal industry cluster at Pulau Muara Besar being built. Vision 2035 cluster strategy still maturing.

name: R&D Expenditure
source: UNESCO Institute for Statistics, WIPO GII, OECD MSTI
comments: R&D expenditure score 4.29, rank 80/135 — very low relative to GDP.
insights: 🟡 Moderate gap vs Singapore (29.04): 24.8 points behind. | ℹ️ R&D expenditure is very low (4.29, rank 80) relative to peers; a clear investment gap.
analysis: R&D Expenditure: Estimated at ~0.1% of GDP — far below UNESCO-recommended 1% minimum and ASEAN average (~1%). GTCI normalised score: 4.29/100. GII 2025 confirms Brunei's knowledge and technology outputs remain critically weak. No R&D tax incentive framework exists. UBD and BRUDIRECT-connected firms conduct limited applied R&D but no national R&D fund.

name: Population covered by at least a 3G Mobile Network
comments: 3G mobile network coverage score 93.09, rank 64/135 — strong infrastructure.
insights: 🟢 Small gap vs Singapore (100.00): 6.9 points behind. | ℹ️ Excellent 3G mobile coverage (93.09, rank 64) — a genuine infrastructure strength.
analysis: ICT Infrastructure: Brunei IDI 2024 score 95.1, ranked 2nd in ASEAN and top 30 globally. Internet access >90% of households. 4G/LTE covers 96%+ of population. 5G launched 2023 (DST, progresif). Fibre broadband expanding. GTCI 3G coverage score 93.09. Strong infrastructure foundation but digital services and skills utilisation lag connectivity levels.

name: Internet access in schools
comments: Not reported in 2025 GTCI for Brunei.
insights: ⚠️ Not reported for Brunei in GTCI 2025 (WEF Executive Opinion Survey data gap).
analysis: Internet in Schools: Score=0 — Brunei not reporting this data to WEF. In practice, most Brunei government schools have internet connectivity (OGN (One Government Network)). Ministry of Education should engage WEF to submit national school ICT infrastructure data and ensure it is reflected in the EOS questionnaire responses from Brunei business leaders.

name: Urbanisation
comments: Urbanisation score 75.76, rank 40/135.
agency: Department of Economic Planning & Statistics - Population Census
insights: 🟡 Moderate gap vs Singapore (100.00): 24.2 points behind. | ℹ️ Urbanisation score of 75.76 (rank 40) trails Singapore's fully urbanised 100.00.
analysis: Urbanisation: Brunei urbanisation rate ~79%. High by ASEAN standards — reflecting small land area and concentration of economic activity in BSB. UN WPP 2024 is the most current data. GTCI normalised score 75.76. Urban concentration supports service delivery but limits agricultural and rural economic diversification.

name: Labour Rights
comments: Labour rights score 74.20, rank 83/135.
agency: Prime Minister's Office/ Ministry of Home Affairs
insights: ✅ Brunei (74.20) meets or exceeds Singapore (73.00). | ℹ️ Good labour rights compliance (74.20, rank 83).
analysis: Labour Rights: ILO ILOSTAT 2024 score 74.2. Brunei has ratified core ILO conventions but gaps remain in freedom of association (Trade Unions Act restricts union rights) and collective bargaining. Employment Order 2009 and its 2023 amendments strengthened worker protections. Migrant worker rights remain an area requiring further improvement.

name: Labour-Employer Cooperation
comments: Labour-employer cooperation score 60.53, rank 60/135.
insights: 🟡 Moderate gap vs Singapore (83.66): 23.1 points behind. | ℹ️ Moderate labour-employer cooperation (60.53, rank 60).
analysis: Labour-Employer Cooperation: WEF EOS 2025 score 60.53/100. Government as dominant employer creates unique dynamics. Private sector cooperation improving under Darussalam Enterprise and BINA (Brunei Industrial Development Authority, now restructured as Business Management Sites under BEDB) initiatives. Tripartite Labour Advisory Board provides consultation mechanism.

name: Professional Management
comments: Professional management score 60.63, rank 61/135.
agency: Department of Economic Planning & Statistics - Economic Census of Enterprises
insights: 🟡 Moderate gap vs Singapore (83.50): 22.9 points behind. | ℹ️ Professional management practices score 60.63 (rank 61) — room to grow toward merit-based systems.
analysis: Professional Management: WEF EOS 2025 score 60.63/100 — improving significantly (+18 pts). Government efforts to professionalise public sector management, IPA (Institut Perkhidmatan Awam, ipa.gov.bn) Executive Development Programmes, and growing corporate governance culture in GLCs contributing to the gain. Sustaining this trajectory requires private sector adoption.

name: Relationship of Pay-Productivity
comments: Relationship of pay to productivity score 46.51, rank 71/135.
insights: 🟡 Moderate gap vs Singapore (65.92): 19.4 points behind. | ℹ️ Pay-productivity linkage is moderate (46.51, rank 71).
analysis: Pay-Productivity Link: WEF EOS 2025 score 46.51/100 — declining. Civil service salary structure insufficiently tied to performance outputs. Private sector productivity incentives limited. Performance management reform in public service (Penilaian Kerja Tahunan system) needs modernisation to tie remuneration more directly to measurable outcomes.

name: Enterprise Software
source: Statista Technology Market Outlook, World Bank WDI
comments: Enterprise software score 11.86, rank 97/135 — minimal adoption; digital transformation gap.
insights: 🟢 Small gap vs Singapore (21.66): 9.8 points behind. | ℹ️ Enterprise software adoption is minimal (11.86, rank 97) — a digital transformation gap.
analysis: Enterprise Software Adoption: Statista estimates Brunei enterprise software market at ~USD 2-3M total. Per-capita spend among the lowest globally. GTCI score 11.86/100. Dominance of public sector (where software procurement is centralised) suppresses commercial market signals. SMEs largely operate without ERP/CRM systems. National digitalisation grants needed urgently.

name: Cloud Computing
source: Statista Technology Market Outlook, World Bank WDI
comments: Cloud computing score 4.79, rank 122/135 — minimal adoption; digital infrastructure gap.
insights: 🔴 Large gap vs Singapore (89.24): 84.5 points behind. | ℹ️ Cloud computing adoption is very low (4.79, rank 122) — a significant digital infrastructure gap.
analysis: Cloud Computing Adoption: Brunei public cloud market nascent — GTCI score 4.79/100. Government agencies beginning cloud migration (AITI cloud strategy). Private sector adoption hindered by data sovereignty concerns, limited local cloud infrastructure, and lack of cloud-competent workforce. National Cloud-First Policy needed with sovereign cloud procurement guidelines.

name: Firms with website
comments: Not reported in 2025 GTCI for Brunei.
ministry: Ministry of Finance and Economy / Ministry of Transport and Infocommunications
insights: ⚠️ Not reported for Brunei in GTCI 2025 (World Bank Enterprise Surveys data gap).
analysis: Business Sophistication (WBES): Score=0 — Brunei last surveyed by World Bank Enterprise Survey in 2015. Data gap means practices around training, corruption, finance access, and competition are not benchmarked. WBES 2025/2026 regional cycle is an opportunity; Ministry of Finance/Economy should formally request Brunei's inclusion. A Brunei-specific business climate survey could bridge the gap.

name: FDI Regulatory Restrictiveness
comments: FDI regulatory restrictiveness score 54.68, rank 77/135.
insights: 🟡 Moderate gap vs Singapore (84.26): 29.6 points behind. | ℹ️ FDI regulatory restrictiveness score of 54.68 (rank 77) suggests room to ease investment barriers.
analysis: FDI Openness: OECD FDI Restrictiveness Index 2024 — Brunei maintains restrictions in financial services, media, and land ownership for foreigners. GTCI score 54.68/100. Despite Brunei's efforts through IPA (Investment Promotions Act, administered by Darussalam Enterprise / de.gov.bn) and the Investment Promotion Authority, actual FDI inflows remain concentrated in O&G. Reducing restrictions in services and tech sectors could unlock new FDI streams.

name: Financial Globalization
source: KOF Swiss Economic Institute Globalization Index
comments: Financial globalisation score 78.93, rank 29/135.
insights: 🟡 Moderate gap vs Singapore (97.20): 18.3 points behind. | ℹ️ Financial globalisation (78.93, rank 29) is reasonably strong but well behind Singapore (97.20).
analysis: Financial Globalisation: KOF 2025 update (covering 2023 data) available. Brunei's financial globalisation score reflects international banking ties (CIMB, BOC, HSBC presence), foreign asset/liability positions, and BIA (Brunei Investment Agency) sovereign wealth fund international portfolio. KOF captures both de facto and de jure aspects of globalisation.

name: Migrant Stock
source: UN Population Division International Migrant Stock
comments: Migrant stock score 49.50, rank 14/135.
insights: 🔴 Large gap vs Singapore (93.18): 43.7 points behind. | ℹ️ Migrant stock score of 49.50 (rank 14) is a relative strength versus most economies.
analysis: Migrant Stock: UN Migrant Stock 2024 — Brunei has ~31% of population as migrants (one of highest shares in ASEAN). However, GTCI normalised score of 49.5 declined by 29 points. Recent tightening of expatriate employment permits and Bruneianisation (Local Business Development Authority) policies may be reducing migrant stock. Rebalancing towards skilled migration could improve score while supporting Wawasan 2035.

name: International Students
source: UNESCO Institute for Statistics / WIPO GII
comments: International students score 15.76, rank 53/135.
ministry: Ministry of Education / Ministry of Home Affairs
agency: Department of Schools / Higher Education Department / Immigration & National Registration Department
insights: ℹ️ Singapore data not reported for this indicator in GTCI 2025; no comparison available. | ℹ️ International student participation is modest (15.76, rank 53).
analysis: International Students: UNESCO UIS — Brunei attracts very few international students. GTCI score 15.76. UBD has some ASEAN Exchange Program students but absolute numbers small. Tuition fees and English-language programs are competitive but scholarship availability for international students is limited. A formal international student attraction strategy (target: 5,000 international students by 2030) could substantially improve this indicator.

name: Brain Gain
comments: Brain gain score 66.32, rank 16/135 — good capacity to attract foreign skilled talent.
insights: 🟢 Small gap vs Singapore (73.22): 6.9 points behind. | ℹ️ Brain gain score of 66.32 (rank 16) reflects a good capacity to attract skilled foreign talent.
analysis: Brain Gain (Talent Attraction): WEF EOS 2025 — Brunei ranks 16th globally on perceived talent attraction. Strong brand as a safe, prosperous, and culturally rich destination resonates with skilled expatriates, particularly from ASEAN, South Asia, and the Philippines. Tax-free income, subsidised fuel and utilities, and stable employment conditions are key draw factors. Opportunity to leverage this brand more actively in global talent recruitment.

name: AI skills migration
comments: Not reported in 2025 GTCI for Brunei.
ministry: Ministry of Transport & Infocommunications (MTIC) / AITI / AITI Digital Skills Programme Office
agency: MTIC: mtic.gov.bn | AITI: aiti.gov.bn | AITI Office: under MTIC
insights: ⚠️ Not reported for Brunei in GTCI 2025 (LinkedIn Economic Graph coverage gap).
analysis: AI Skills Migration: LinkedIn Economic Graph 2024 — Brunei not covered. Indicator measures net migration of AI-skilled professionals via LinkedIn platform data. Brunei's LinkedIn penetration is moderate but AI-specific talent movement data is unavailable for this small market. Ministry of Transport & Infocommunications (MTIC)/AITI should engage LinkedIn Economic Graph team for Brunei data inclusion.

name: Tolerance of minorities
source: The Fund for Peace. Fragile States Index 2024
comments: Tolerance of minorities score 27.66, rank 93/135 — requires policy attention for inclusivity.
insights: 🔴 Large gap vs Singapore (73.40): 45.7 points behind. | ℹ️ Tolerance of minorities scores low (27.66, rank 93) — a clear area for policy attention.
analysis: Group Grievances / Minority Tolerance: FSI 2024 (May 2024) — Brunei Group Grievances sub-indicator at 27.66. Reflects perceptions around treatment of religious minorities (non-Muslims represent ~33% of population), rights of non-citizens, and ethnic minority recognition. FSI uses triangulated data from media, reports, and expert assessment. Brunei's MIB (Melayu Islam Beraja) national philosophy creates structural tensions with minority inclusion indicators.

name: Tolerance of immigrant
comments: Not reported in 2025 GTCI for Brunei.
insights: ⚠️ Not reported for Brunei in GTCI 2025 (Gallup World Poll coverage gap).
analysis: Tolerance of Immigrants (Gallup): Score=0 — Brunei last included in Gallup fieldwork ~2020. Actual public tolerance levels in Brunei are unmeasured by this global benchmark. Given the ~31% migrant population, tolerance attitudes are important for social cohesion. Ministry of Home Affairs could commission an annual national perception survey using Gallup-compatible methodology to build a data series for GTCI submission.

name: Social Mobility
comments: Social mobility score 60.68, rank 52/135.
insights: 🟡 Moderate gap vs Singapore (81.05): 20.4 points behind. | ℹ️ Social mobility score of 60.68 (rank 52) is moderate.
analysis: Social Mobility: WEF EOS 2025 score 60.68 — improving significantly (+24 pts). Brunei's welfare state model (free healthcare, subsidised housing, universal education scholarships) supports upward social mobility. Government scholarship programs (MKMA, JPA, BESERAH) enable children from all backgrounds to access higher education. Wawasan 2035 social equity targets reinforcing this trend.

name: Women Economic Empowerment
source: World Bank Women, Business and the Law 2024
comments: Economic empowerment of women score 53.13, rank 126/135 — Brunei's lowest-ranked indicator; a critical gender gap.
ministry: Ministry of Culture, Youth and Sports / Ministry of Finance and Economy
insights: 🟡 Moderate gap vs Singapore (82.50): 29.4 points behind. | ℹ️ Economic empowerment of women (53.13) ranks 126th globally — the lowest-ranked indicator for Brunei in GTCI 2025, signalling a critical gender gap.
analysis: Gender Economic Empowerment (WBL): WBL 2024 score 53.12/100 (rank 126/190). WBL 2.0 breakdown: Legal Frameworks 35.0, Supportive Frameworks 24.2, Expert Opinions 91.3. Low scores in Mobility, Workplace, Marriage and Parenthood dimensions. Key gaps: no equal pay legislation, limited maternity leave mandates in private sector, restrictions on women's property rights. WBL 2026 expected March 2026. Expert opinion (91.3) far exceeds legal score (35.0) — suggesting practice ahead of law.

name: Gender Parity High-Skilled Jobs
comments: Gender parity in high-skilled jobs score 82.62, rank 48/135 — a strength.
insights: ✅ Brunei (82.62) meets or exceeds Singapore (81.10). | ℹ️ Strong gender parity in high-skilled jobs (82.62, rank 48).
analysis: Gender Parity in High-Skilled Jobs: ILO ILOSTAT 2024-2025 — Brunei women constitute a strong share of professional and managerial workforce. Brunei LFS 2024 confirms female workers account for ~85% of medium and high-skill occupational categories. Strong female participation in education, healthcare, and government sectors. Private sector female leadership less prominent but improving.

name: Leadership opportunities for women
comments: Leadership opportunities for women score 71.54, rank 28/135.
insights: 🟡 Moderate gap vs Singapore (84.94): 13.4 points behind. | ℹ️ Leadership opportunities for women score well (71.54, rank 28).
analysis: Women in Leadership: WEF EOS 2025 — Brunei ranks 28th globally (rank 28 out of 135). Senior female public servants, female HM Appointees to LegCo, and prominent women in education and healthcare leadership contribute to positive perception. Cultural and religious norms create some glass ceilings in private sector C-suite roles. Rank 28 is Brunei's strongest social inclusion indicator.

name: Vocational Enrolment
comments: Vocational enrolment score 20.87, rank 56/135 — low participation; more STEM/technical pathways needed.
insights: ℹ️ Singapore data not reported for this indicator in GTCI 2025; no comparison available. | ℹ️ Vocational enrolment is low (20.87, rank 56) — more STEM/technical pathways are needed.
analysis: Vocational Enrolment: UNESCO UIS 2023 — Vocational enrolment as % of secondary education is low in Brunei. IBTE (Institute of Brunei Technical Education) is the main TVET provider with 5 campuses. Total TVET enrolment ~8,000 students. Low relative to secondary school population (~30,000). Stigma against vocational pathways persists. Brunei Skills Development Centre (BSDC) and SPN21 TVET stream aiming to change this.

name: Tertiary Enrolment
source: UNESCO Institute for Statistics / WIPO GII
comments: Tertiary enrolment score 20.56, rank 85/135 — a significant access gap.
insights: 🔴 Large gap vs Singapore (58.13): 37.6 points behind. | ℹ️ Tertiary enrolment (20.56, rank 85) is among the weakest indicators for Brunei — a significant access gap.
analysis: Tertiary Enrolment: UNESCO UIS — Brunei tertiary GER ~27% (2019 most recent confirmed). Extremely low despite top-6 spending per student. Root cause: many Bruneians study abroad (Malaysia, UK, Australia) on government scholarships; local university capacity limited to UBD, UTB, UNISSA, Laksamana, and Cosmopolitan College. Expanding hybrid/online programs and increasing local university capacity are critical levers.

name: Tertiary Education Expenditure
comments: Tertiary education expenditure score 50.31, rank 6/135 — among the highest per-student spenders globally.
insights: ✅ Brunei (50.31) meets or exceeds Singapore (35.95). | ℹ️ Tertiary education expenditure is exceptionally high (50.31, rank 6) — Brunei outspends Singapore here.
analysis: Education Expenditure per Student: UNESCO UIS 2024 — Brunei government tertiary spending per student is among the highest globally (rank 6). Full scholarships (MKMA, JPA), OS (overseas scholarship) program, and overseas sponsorships drive high per-student costs. Despite top-6 investment, quality outcomes in tertiary enrolment (rank 85) and research output do not yet match the financial commitment.

name: PISA Scores (Reading/Math/Science)
source: OECD Programme for International Student Assessment 2022
comments: PISA-based reading/maths/science score 42.06, rank 43/135.
insights: 🔴 Large gap vs Singapore (91.94): 49.9 points behind. | ℹ️ PISA-based learning outcomes score 42.06 (rank 43) — a moderate but improvable result.
analysis: Educational Quality (PISA): PISA 2022 results (Dec 2023) — Brunei ranked 43rd among 81 participating education systems in combined Reading, Mathematics, and Science. Scores improving from 2018 baseline. Gap to Singapore (4th in PISA 2022) remains significant (~100 points in maths). PISA 2025 cycle currently underway; Brunei participating. Results expected December 2026. MOE curriculum reforms under SPN21 contributing to gradual improvement.

name: University Ranking
comments: University ranking score 17.53, rank 53/135 — limited representation in global rankings.
insights: 🔴 Large gap vs Singapore (69.70): 52.2 points behind. | ℹ️ University ranking performance is weak (17.53, rank 53), reflecting limited representation in global rankings.
source_score: QS World Rank 367; THE Rank 351 (GTCI-normalised rank: 53/135)
analysis: University Rankings: QS WUR 2026 (June 2025) — UBD ranked 367th globally, up 18 places from 385 (2025). Academic Reputation rose 32 places to 406th globally. THE 2026: UBD ranked 351st (up from 401-500 band). UTB entered THE rankings (601+) for first time in 2025. QS Asia rank: 78th. Two consecutive years of improvement post-methodology overhaul. GTCI normalised score 17.53 will improve with next update.

name: Business Masters Education
source: Own computation based on QS (2025) QS Global MBA and Business Masters Rankings 2025
comments: Score 0.00 reflects the GTCI normalisation floor; Brunei ranked 64/135. No Brunei business school currently appears in the QS Global MBA and Business Masters Rankings.
insights: 🔴 Large gap vs Singapore (61.94): 61.9 points behind. | ℹ️ Business masters education scores at the GTCI normalisation floor (0.00, rank 64/135): no Brunei business school currently appears in the QS Global MBA rankings.
analysis: Business School Quality (MBA Rankings): QS Global MBA Rankings 2025 and 2026 confirm no Brunei business school is ranked. UBD School of Business and Economics (UBDSBE) offers MBA programs but lacks critical mass in research output, international faculty, and alumni network to qualify for global rankings. Pathway: partner with ranked international business schools (e.g., INSEAD Southeast Asia) for joint program delivery and faculty exchange.

name: Prevalence of training in firms
comments: Not reported in 2025 GTCI for Brunei.
insights: ⚠️ Not reported for Brunei in GTCI 2025 (World Bank Enterprise Surveys data gap).
analysis: Firms Offering Training: World Bank Enterprise Surveys — Brunei last surveyed 2015. Data on training practices by Bruneian firms is absent from global benchmarks. WBES 2025/2026 regional cycle. Ministry of Finance and Economy should submit a formal request to the World Bank WBES team for Brunei inclusion. Alternatively, BEDB/Darussalam Enterprise could conduct a national business climate survey capturing this metric.

name: Employee Development
comments: Employee development score 62.24, rank 57/135 — moderate training investment.
insights: 🟡 Moderate gap vs Singapore (72.64): 10.4 points behind. | ℹ️ Employee development investment is moderate (62.24, rank 57).
analysis: Employee Development: WEF EOS 2025 score 62.24/100. IPA (Institut Perkhidmatan Awam, ipa.gov.bn) provides structured learning for public servants. Private sector L&D investment remains low — few large companies have dedicated training budgets. HRD-Corp equivalent (like Malaysia's levy-funded training system) does not exist in Brunei. Introduction of a skills levy system could incentivise private sector training investment.

name: Delegation of Authority
comments: Delegation of authority score 65.69, rank 26/135 — a management strength.
insights: 🟢 Small gap vs Singapore (67.27): 1.6 points behind. | ℹ️ Delegation of authority is a relative strength (65.69, rank 26).
analysis: Delegation of Authority: WEF EOS 2025 — Brunei ranks 26th globally (score 65.69). Business executives rate delegation practices positively. Flat management structures in some GLCs and professional services firms. Government-linked companies increasingly empowering mid-level management as part of corporate governance modernisation. Strong result for Brunei — should be showcased in employer branding.

name: Youth Inclusion (NEET)
comments: Youth inclusion (NEET) score 67.31, rank 66/135.
insights: 🟡 Moderate gap vs Singapore (92.99): 25.7 points behind. | ℹ️ Youth inclusion / NEET score of 67.31 (rank 66) is moderate.
analysis: Youth NEET Rate: ILO ILOSTAT + Brunei LFS 2025 (released April 2026) — Youth NEET (Not in Education, Employment or Training) rate improving. Employed population 220,400 (LFS 2025). DEPS/Department of Labour employment support programs, HRD subsidies, and graduate placement initiatives reducing NEET rates. Youth unemployment remains structural challenge — particularly for non-degree holders in a credential-heavy labour market.

name: Use of virtual social networks
comments: Use of virtual social networks score 63.50, rank 70/135.
insights: 🟡 Moderate gap vs Singapore (87.96): 24.5 points behind. | ℹ️ Use of virtual social networks is moderate (63.50, rank 70).
analysis: Virtual Social Networks for Business: WEF EOS 2025 score 63.5. High internet penetration (90%+) and smartphone usage support social media adoption. LinkedIn, WhatsApp Business, and Facebook are primary business networking tools. Formal use of social networks for professional development and B2B networking growing but not yet systematic. Digital business culture maturing.

name: Use of virtual professional networks
comments: Use of virtual professional networks score 36.47, rank 35/135.
insights: 🔴 Large gap vs Singapore (80.52): 44.0 points behind. | ℹ️ Use of virtual professional networks is low (36.47, rank 35 — relative ranking reflects normalisation across a constrained data set), pointing to a LinkedIn/professional-network penetration gap versus Singapore.
analysis: LinkedIn Connectivity: LinkedIn Economic Graph 2023 data — Brunei ranks 35th globally in professional networking penetration among workforce. Higher than expected for a nation of 450,000. Growing use among graduates, white-collar professionals, and government officials. LinkedIn Talent Insights available for Brunei market. LinkedIn's 2025 data update will reflect further growth as younger Bruneians join the workforce.

name: Pension Coverage
source: International Labour Organization Social Security Inquiry
comments: Pension coverage score 100.00, rank 1/135 — universal coverage; one of Brunei's two world #1 indicators.
ministry: Ministry of Finance and Economy / Tabung Amanah Pekerja / Ministry of Culture, Youth and Sports
insights: ✅ Brunei (100.00) meets or exceeds Singapore (36.73). | ℹ️ Pension coverage is universal (100.00) — Brunei's joint world #1 ranking and a genuine retention strength (Singapore scores far lower here, 36.73, rank 93).
analysis: Pension Coverage: ILO 2024 confirms Brunei TAP (Tabung Amanah Pekerja) and SCP (Supplemental Contributory Pension) provide 100% pension coverage for all formal sector workers. All employed Bruneian nationals covered by mandatory contributory pension scheme. World #1 achievement globally. A key competitive advantage for talent retention messaging — Brunei offers comprehensive retirement security to all workers.

name: Social Protection
comments: Social protection score 50.00, rank 59/135.
ministry: Ministry of Finance and Economy / Tabung Amanah Pekerja / Ministry of Culture, Youth and Sports
insights: 🟡 Moderate gap vs Singapore (67.73): 17.7 points behind. | ℹ️ Social protection score of 50.00 (rank 59) suggests room to strengthen safety nets.
analysis: Social Protection Perception: WEF EOS 2025 score 50/100 — moderate and declining. Despite comprehensive government welfare programs (subsidised housing, healthcare, education, fuel), business leaders' perception of social safety net effectiveness is middling. Informal sector workers and migrant workers feel less protected. Communication of social protection benefits and broader coverage of non-citizen workers could improve this score.

name: Brain Retention
comments: Brain retention score 48.17, rank 61/135.
insights: 🔴 Large gap vs Singapore (79.95): 31.8 points behind. | ℹ️ Brain retention is moderate (48.17, rank 61).
analysis: Brain Retention: WEF EOS 2025 score 48.17 — improving. Business executives perceive talent increasingly choosing to remain in Brunei. Competitive salaries in O&G and government, subsidised cost of living, and quality lifestyle factors (safety, food, community) support retention. Awareness of overseas opportunities via social media creates competitive pressure. Targeted retention programs for high-skilled nationals studying abroad needed.

name: Environmental Performance
source: Yale Center for Environmental Law & Policy EPI 2024
comments: Environmental performance score 48.30, rank 55/135.
agency: Department of Environment, Parks and Recreation
insights: 🟢 Small gap vs Singapore (53.00): 4.7 points behind. | ℹ️ Environmental performance (48.30, rank 55) is broadly comparable to Singapore (53.00).
analysis: Environmental Performance: Yale EPI 2024 — Brunei scored 48.3/100. Performs above Asia-Pacific average in water/sanitation and air quality. Below average in climate change mitigation (high carbon intensity from O&G sector), ecosystem services, and biodiversity. Brunei's Long-Term Development Strategy 2035 includes green economy components. Energy transition from fossil fuels critical to improving EPI score. EPI 2026 will reflect recent renewable energy investments.

name: Vulnerable Employment
comments: Vulnerable employment score 95.02, rank 10/135 — very low vulnerable employment; strong job security.
insights: ✅ Brunei (95.02) meets or exceeds Singapore (89.78). | ℹ️ Vulnerable employment is very low (95.02, rank 10) — a strong indicator of job security.
analysis: Vulnerable Employment: ILO ILOSTAT 2024 — Vulnerable employment (own-account + contributing family workers as % of total) very low in Brunei (rank ~10 globally). Government is the largest employer; public sector jobs are stable and protected. Strong formal employment culture. GTCI score 95.02. However, migrant workers in construction and domestic work may be in more precarious situations not fully captured.

name: Protect against future disasters
comments: Not reported in 2025 GTCI for Brunei.
insights: ⚠️ Not reported for Brunei in GTCI 2025 (Lloyd's Register World Risk Poll coverage gap).
analysis: Workplace Safety Perception: LRF World Risk Poll 2024 (published Oct 2024) covers 121 countries — Brunei not included. WRP surveys ~125,000 people globally. Brunei's small population and limited survey participation explains absence. In practice, Brunei's workplace safety record (monitored by DOSH — Department of Occupational Safety and Health) is good. Engaging LRF for Brunei inclusion in WRP 2027 could secure a positive score based on actual safety conditions.

name: Household financial resilience
comments: Not reported in 2025 GTCI for Brunei.
agency: Department of Economic Planning & Statistics - Household Expenditure Survey
insights: ⚠️ Not reported for Brunei in GTCI 2025 (Global Findex Database coverage gap).
analysis: Financial Inclusion: Global Findex 2024 (Oct 2024) — Brunei not included in 123-economy survey. BDCB (Brunei Darussalam Central Bank, formerly AMBD, renamed June 2021, bdcb.gov.bn) and BDCB data shows high banking penetration among Bruneian nationals but data not submitted to Findex. Ministry of Finance & Economy should coordinate with World Bank for Brunei inclusion in Findex 2027. Based on national data, Brunei account ownership is estimated >90% among nationals.

name: Personal rights
comments: Not reported in 2025 GTCI for Brunei.
insights: ⚠️ Not reported for Brunei in GTCI 2025 (Social Progress Imperative coverage gap).
analysis: Personal Rights: Social Progress Index 2024 — Brunei not included in ~170-country index. SPI assesses political rights, freedom of expression, assembly, and movement. Brunei's MIB constitutional framework, absence of political parties, and press freedom limitations would likely result in a moderate SPI personal rights score if assessed. Government should proactively engage Social Progress Imperative to facilitate Brunei data inclusion.

name: Personal safety
comments: Not reported in 2025 GTCI for Brunei.
insights: ⚠️ Not reported for Brunei in GTCI 2025 (Social Progress Imperative coverage gap).
analysis: Personal Safety: SPI 2024 — Brunei not included. Brunei is empirically one of the safest countries in Southeast Asia (extremely low crime rates, no street gang issues, low domestic violence reported). Royal Brunei Police Force crime statistics would support a high personal safety score if submitted. PDRM/Ministry of Home Affairs should engage SPI to secure Brunei inclusion — this is a missed opportunity to showcase a genuine national strength.

name: Physician density
comments: Physician density score 27.37, rank 77/135.
insights: 🟡 Moderate gap vs Singapore (43.44): 16.1 points behind. | ℹ️ Physician density is moderate (27.37, rank 77).
analysis: Physician Density: WHO GHO + Brunei MOH Annual Report 2023 — 1.87 physicians per 1,000 population. Below OECD average (~3.7) but above ASEAN average (~1.4). RIPAS Hospital (national referral hospital) and district health centres form backbone of public health system. Brunei relies significantly on expatriate medical professionals. MOH medical training intake expanding; overseas medical scholars on bond expected to increase physician density by 2030.

name: Sanitation
comments: Sanitation score 98.00, rank 43/135 — excellent basic sanitation infrastructure.
insights: 🟢 Small gap vs Singapore (100.00): 2.0 points behind. | ℹ️ Sanitation coverage is excellent (98.00, rank 43), close to Singapore's perfect score.
analysis: Sanitation Coverage: JMP 2023 — Brunei sanitation coverage near-universal (98%+). Safely managed sanitation and clean water access are exemplary — consistent top-5 global performance. All urban areas and most rural areas connected to piped water and sewerage. One of Brunei's strongest social infrastructure achievements. Relevant for \"Retain\" pillar quality-of-life messaging in talent attraction campaigns.

name: Employee Wellbeing
comments: Not reported in 2025 GTCI for Brunei.
ministry: Ministry of Health / Ministry of Culture, Youth and Sports
insights: ⚠️ Not reported for Brunei in GTCI 2025 (Gallup World Poll coverage gap).
analysis: Employee Wellbeing: Gallup World Poll Wellbeing module — Brunei not covered. Score=0. Gallup's global wellbeing surveys require representative national samples with in-country fieldwork partners. Brunei's wellbeing is understood to be high based on national surveys (high life satisfaction, low stress indicators). Ministry of Finance Statistics Department could commission a National Wellbeing Survey using Gallup-compatible methodology to build a data series for future GTCI submissions.

name: Workforce Secondary Education
comments: Workforce with secondary education score 62.63, rank 28/135.
insights: ✅ Brunei (62.63) meets or exceeds Singapore (11.65). | ℹ️ Workforce with secondary education scores 62.63 (rank 28) — notably, this is one of the few indicators where Brunei substantially outranks Singapore (rank 117), largely reflecting differences in how each country's labour force composition is measured.
analysis: Workforce Secondary Education: ILOSTAT + LFS 2025 — Workforce with secondary education as % declining in Brunei. Score 62.63 (-10.6 pts). This seemingly counterintuitive decline reflects a compositional shift: younger cohorts with tertiary education are replacing older secondary-educated workers, temporarily reducing the secondary-only share. The trend is actually positive — indicating upskilling — but appears as a decline in this specific metric.

name: Population Secondary Education
comments: Population with secondary education score 63.94, rank 27/135.
insights: ✅ Brunei (63.94) meets or exceeds Singapore (36.97). | ℹ️ Population with secondary education (63.94, rank 27) is a solid result.
analysis: Population Secondary Education Attainment (25+): ILOSTAT 2025 — 63.94% of Brunei population aged 25+ has at least secondary education. Improving as younger, better-educated cohorts age into the 25+ demographic. Government investment in universal secondary education through SPN21 system is yielding demographic dividends. Gap to Singapore (~85%) reflects legacy of lower historical education access.

name: Technicians & Associate Professionals
comments: Technicians and associate professionals score 57.06, rank 36/135.
insights: 🟡 Moderate gap vs Singapore (85.41): 28.4 points behind. | ℹ️ Technicians and associate professionals score 57.06 (rank 36), exceeding Singapore's 52.43.
analysis: Technicians & Associate Professionals: ILOSTAT 2025 — share of technicians and associate professionals in Brunei workforce growing (+6.9 pts). IBTE graduates contributing to this pool. Brunei Energy Sector demand for technical talent (petroleum technicians, lab analysts) driving growth. Growing demand in digital economy for network technicians, data analysts, and cybersecurity associates presents further opportunity.

name: Labour Productivity per Employee
source: The Conference Board, Total Economy Database
comments: Not reported in 2025 GTCI for Brunei.
insights: ⚠️ Not reported for Brunei in GTCI 2025 (Conference Board Total Economy Database coverage gap).
analysis: Labour Productivity: Conference Board TED — Brunei not reported. Score=0. TED covers ~75 major economies. Brunei's labour productivity (GDP per employed person) can be estimated from DEPS national accounts data: high due to O&G sector but unrepresentative of overall economic productivity. DEPS should submit labour productivity estimates directly to Conference Board for inclusion in TED methodology.

name: Finding Skilled Employees
comments: Ease of finding skilled employees score 42.88, rank 119/135 — one of the most challenging indicators for Brunei; signals a critical skills shortage.
insights: 🟡 Moderate gap vs Singapore (58.33): 15.4 points behind. | ℹ️ Ease of finding skilled employees is the most challenging indicator for Brunei (42.88, rank 119) — a critical, widely-cited skills shortage signal.
analysis: Finding Skilled Employees: WEF EOS 2025 — Brunei ranks 119/135 globally — worst performing GTCI indicator and worst in ASEAN. Business leaders report severe difficulty finding qualified local talent across IT, engineering, finance, and management. Structural causes: low tertiary enrolment, skills mismatch between education output and employer needs, over-reliance on public sector as employer of graduates, and limited private sector internship/apprenticeship pathways. CRITICAL priority.

name: Education System Relevance
comments: Relevance of education system to the economy score 60.78, rank 26/135.
insights: 🟡 Moderate gap vs Singapore (77.26): 16.5 points behind. | ℹ️ Education system relevance to the economy scores 60.78 (rank 26) — a moderate alignment.
analysis: Education-Business Relevance: WEF EOS 2025 score 60.78. Curriculum improvements underway (SPN21 reforms, IBTE industry alignment) but gap between graduates' competencies and private sector requirements persists. UBD Industry Advisory Boards attempting to bridge gap. Internship requirements for all university programs (IBTE mandate since 2022) contributing to improvement.

name: Skills Matching
comments: Skills matching score 65.70, rank 59/135.
insights: ℹ️ Singapore data not reported for this indicator in GTCI 2025; no comparison available. | ℹ️ Skills matching scores 65.70 (rank 59) — a moderate result.
analysis: Skills Matching: WEF EOS 2025 score 65.7. JobCentre Brunei (managed by Department of Labour, Ministry of Home Affairs, labour.gov.bn) matching platform improving graduate-to-job placement rates. Brunei Skills Recognition Framework (BSRF) under development to standardise skills credentials. Private sector placement challenging due to salary expectation gaps between government and corporate sector. Expansion of apprenticeship programs recommended.

name: Educated Unemployment
comments: Highly educated unemployment score 83.21, rank 48/135 — low unemployment among the educated.
insights: 🟢 Small gap vs Singapore (90.64): 7.4 points behind. | ℹ️ Highly educated unemployment is low (83.21, rank 48), indicating a relatively good job market for graduates.
analysis: Educated Unemployment: ILOSTAT + LFS 2024 — Educated unemployment (graduates unemployed as % of educated labour force) low in Brunei (rank 48). Brunei LFS 2024: overall unemployment 4.7%. Graduate employment rate relatively high due to public sector absorption. However, graduate underemployment (working below qualification level) is a concern not captured in this metric — potentially significant in Brunei given public sector dominance.

name: Workforce with Tertiary Education
comments: Workforce with tertiary education score 35.56, rank 62/135.
insights: 🔴 Large gap vs Singapore (89.67): 54.1 points behind. | ℹ️ Workforce with tertiary education is limited (35.56, rank 62).
analysis: Workforce Tertiary Education: ILOSTAT + LFS 2025 — 35.56% of Brunei employed workforce holds tertiary qualification. Growing as university graduates enter labour market. Below Singapore (~60%) and Malaysia (~45%). Accelerating tertiary enrolment and retaining overseas graduates are key levers. Government overseas scholarship graduates (bonded) are increasingly returning and entering workforce.

name: Soft Skills
comments: Soft skills score 57.13, rank 60/135.
insights: 🟡 Moderate gap vs Singapore (68.19): 11.1 points behind. | ℹ️ Soft skills score is mid-range (57.13, rank 60) — room for improvement.
analysis: Soft Skills: WEF EOS 2024 (note: GTCI 2025 uses 2024 EOS for this indicator; 2025 EOS will feed GTCI 2026) — score 57.13. Communication, critical thinking, creativity, adaptability among Brunei workers rated moderate. SPN21 curriculum reform targets 21st-century competencies. Private sector cites soft skills (particularly communication in English and problem-solving) as key gap among fresh graduates. Language skills development critical.

name: Professionals
source: International Labour Organization ILOSTAT; World Bank, Global Jobs Indicators Database (JOIN)
comments: Professionals score 33.81, rank 45/135.
insights: 🟡 Moderate gap vs Singapore (51.85): 18.0 points behind. | ℹ️ Professional employment share is moderate (33.81, rank 45).
analysis: Professionals in Workforce: ILOSTAT + WDI 2025 — Professionals (ISCO major group 2) as % of Brunei workforce at 33.81. Healthcare professionals, educators, and engineers dominant. IT professionals growing from small base. Brunei's small total workforce (~222,000) limits absolute numbers. Diversification of professional categories through Digital Economy Masterplan 2025 (DE25) and healthcare expansion programs will increase this ratio.

name: Researchers, Full-time equivalent
source: Global Innovation Index Database, WIPO 2025; UNESCO Institute for Statistics (UIS); OECD MSTI Database
comments: Researchers (FTE) score 4.82, rank 73/135 — very low relative to population.
insights: 🔴 Large gap vs Singapore (82.79): 78.0 points behind. | ℹ️ Full-time-equivalent researcher density is very low (4.82, rank 73) relative to population.
analysis: Researcher Density: GII 2025 (Sep 2025) — Brunei researcher FTE density extremely low (0.2-0.3 per 1,000 labour force; GTCI normalised score 4.82, rank 73). GII 2025 confirms Brunei ranked 88th overall with continued weakness in knowledge outputs. Without researchers, R&D output and IP generation remain minimal. Establishing a Brunei Research Council, creating competitive researcher salaries, and introducing researcher visa pathways are essential.

name: Senior Officials and Managers
source: International Labour Organization ILOSTAT; World Bank, Global Jobs Indicators Database (JOIN)
comments: Senior officials and managers score 45.11, rank 14/135 — a genuine strength.
ministry: Prime Minister's Office / Ministry of Finance and Economy
agency: Civil Service Commission / Department of Economic Planning & Statistics
insights: 🔴 Large gap vs Singapore (86.42): 41.3 points behind. | ℹ️ Senior officials and managers score 45.11 (rank 14) — a genuine strength, reflecting strong representation in management roles.
analysis: Senior Officials & Managers: ILOSTAT + WDI 2025 — Senior officials and managers as % of workforce ranks Brunei 14th globally. Bruneians occupy large share of managerial roles across public sector, GLCs, and some private firms. Bumiputra Malay business ownership policy (local content requirements) ensures Bruneian management presence. Private sector management quality improvements underway through IPA leadership programmes and MBA scholarship graduates.

name: Digital Skills
comments: Digital skills score 100.00, rank 1/135 — one of Brunei's two world #1 indicators (joint with Singapore).
insights: ✅ Brunei (100.00) meets or exceeds Singapore (50.66). | ℹ️ Brunei posts a perfect digital skills score (100.00), matching Singapore for joint world #1 — note this is a high-level composite score and should be read alongside lower scores on related output indicators such as software development (3.02) and mobile app development (43.27).
source_score: IDI 95.1 (ITU, 2nd in ASEAN); GTCI-normalised rank 1/135 (joint world #1 with Singapore)
analysis: Digital Skills (ITU): ITU IDI 2024 — Brunei scored 95.1, ranked 2nd in ASEAN. 2025 IDI published. GTCI normalised score: 100/100. Note: the perfect score may reflect ITU's ICT access/use proxy for digital skills rather than directly measuring user competency. Brunei's strong connectivity infrastructure (5G, fibre) drives IDI score but actual digital skills competency (measured by OECD PIAAC or national digital literacy assessments) may differ. A national digital skills assessment would provide clearer evidence.

name: AI Talent Concentration
source: LinkedIn Economic Graph; OECD AI Policy Observatory
comments: Not reported in 2025 GTCI for Brunei.
ministry: Ministry of Transport & Infocommunications (MTIC) / AITI / AITI Digital Skills Programme Office
insights: ⚠️ Not reported for Brunei in GTCI 2025 (LinkedIn Economic Graph / OECD AI Policy Observatory coverage gap).
analysis: AI Talent Concentration: New indicator in GTCI 2025. Brunei not covered. Measures share of AI-skilled professionals as % of workforce via LinkedIn data. Brunei's AI talent ecosystem is nascent: AITI's Digital Economy Masterplan 2025 (DE25) (2025-2030) includes AI competency development targets. University AI courses at UBD and UTB launching 2025. LinkedIn engagement and AI upskilling programs will be essential to appear in future versions of this indicator.

name: ICT Services Exports
source: Global Innovation Index Database, WIPO 2025; WTO/UNCTAD Trade in Commercial Services Database
comments: ICT services exports score 2.44, rank 112/135 — minimal sector; economic diversification gap.
insights: 🟡 Moderate gap vs Singapore (24.13): 21.7 points behind. | ℹ️ ICT services exports are minimal (2.44, rank 112) — a clear economic diversification gap.
analysis: ICT Services Exports: WTO Trade Stats + GII 2025 — Brunei ICT services exports extremely low (GTCI score 2.44, rank 112/135). Brunei's export base remains >90% dominated by LNG and crude oil. ICT services (software, IT consulting, digital services, BPO) represent <1% of service exports. GII 2025 ranks Brunei 124th in Creative Outputs. Deliberate ICT export diversification policy — targeting ASEAN digital services market — is the primary lever.

name: Mobile App Development
source: Global Innovation Index Database, WIPO 2025; IMF World Economic Outlook Database
comments: Mobile app development score 43.27, rank 112/135.
insights: 🔴 Large gap vs Singapore (84.52): 41.2 points behind. | ℹ️ Mobile app development activity is low (43.27, rank 112).
analysis: Mobile App Development: GII 2025 via Sensor Tower data — Brunei at 43.27/100 (moderate). Some local app development activity but limited commercial scale. Government apps (BruHealth, SALPOP) well-adopted domestically. Gaming, fintech, and e-government apps being developed. Digital Economy Masterplan 2025 (DE25) encourages app ecosystem growth through AppSprint and StartUp Brunei competitions.

name: Intellectual Property Receipts
source: IMF Balance of Payments Statistics; WTO Trade Database
comments: Score 0.00 reflects the GTCI normalisation floor; Brunei ranked 121/135 — near-zero IP licensing receipts from abroad.
insights: 🔴 Large gap vs Singapore (72.97): 73.0 points behind. | ℹ️ Intellectual property receipts sit at the GTCI normalisation floor (0.00, rank 121) — IP licensing income from abroad is near zero.
analysis: IP Receipts: IMF BOP 2024 confirms Brunei reports zero intellectual property receipts. No royalties, licensing fees, or patent income earned from abroad. WIPO GII 2025 ranks Brunei 124th in Creative Outputs. Reflects absence of IP-generating industries. Brunei Intellectual Property Office (BruIPO) established 2013 but patent filing rates remain extremely low. Introduction of IP tax incentives (Patent Box regime) and commercialisation support for UBD/UTB research outputs are critical.

name: High-Value Exports
source: UN Comtrade; World Bank WDI; OECD classification
comments: High-value exports score 1.53, rank 115/135 — reflects continued reliance on oil and gas.
insights: 🔴 Large gap vs Singapore (87.74): 86.2 points behind. | ℹ️ High-value exports are minimal (1.53, rank 115), reflecting continued reliance on oil and gas.
analysis: High-Value Exports: UN Comtrade + WDI 2024 — High-value exports (medium-high tech manufacturing + ICT + knowledge-intensive services) represent <5% of total Brunei exports. O&G dominance constrains export complexity (Economic Complexity Index: Brunei rank ~120). GII 2025: Brunei 115th in Knowledge & Technology Outputs. Halal food and cosmetics export value chain, precision engineering, and digital services are priority diversification targets under BEDB's sector development programs.

name: Software Development
source: GitHub via WIPO GII 2025; UN World Population Prospects 2024
comments: Software development score 3.02, rank 96/135 (GitHub-derived measure).
insights: 🔴 Large gap vs Singapore (100.00): 97.0 points behind. | ℹ️ Software development activity is low (3.02, rank 96), based on the methodology's GitHub-derived measure.
analysis: Software Development: GitHub data via GII 2025 — Brunei software development activity minimal (score 3.02). GitHub commits per capita extremely low. Local developer community small but growing — BruDev community, BruHack hackathon series, and AITI coding bootcamps nurturing talent. Open-source contributions and startup tech products slowly emerging. DE25 targets doubling tech startup numbers by 2028.

name: New Business Density
comments: Score 0.00 reflects the GTCI normalisation floor; Brunei ranked 125/135 — among the lowest-ranked indicators for Brunei.
insights: 🔴 Large gap vs Singapore (46.56): 46.6 points behind. | ℹ️ New business density sits at the GTCI normalisation floor (0.00, rank 125) — among the lowest-ranked indicators for Brunei.
analysis: New Business Density: World Bank Entrepreneurship Database — Brunei last reported 2022; score=0 in GTCI 2025. CRITICAL DATA ACTION: Brunei ROCBN registered 6,570 new enterprises in 2023 and estimated 7,200+ in 2024. This data must be submitted to the World Bank Entrepreneurship Database before GTCI 2026 data cutoff. Ministry of Finance & Economy / ROCBN should contact World Bank for urgent data submission. This single action could significantly improve Brunei's rank in the Growth (Grow) pillar.

name: Scientific Journal Articles
source: World Bank, World Development Indicators based on National Science Foundation, Science and Engineering Indicators; population data come from World Bank, World Development Indicators
comments: Scientific journal articles score 32.91, rank 37/135.
insights: 🔴 Large gap vs Singapore (81.54): 48.6 points behind. | ℹ️ Scientific journal article output scores 32.91 (rank 37) — a comparatively encouraging research-output indicator.
analysis: Scientific Publications: Scopus/SCImago 2024 + UNESCO Science Report — Brunei scientific publications growing; rank 37/135 globally (+8.62 pts improvement). UBD driving publication output through research institutes (CIBM, ISLB, IEE). UTB and UNISSA also contributing. Nature Index 2025 shows UBD rising in health sciences. International collaboration (with UK, Australia, Malaysia) boosting citation impact. Target: maintain rank improvement with sustained research investment and open-access publication policy.
